comments.title: Am I doing this rite right?
comments.content:
Hi, John.
OK – good news is that my brain finally got over the surprise of having copy 440 of 550 of The Dolman Arch appear on the doorstep of Tower 440, GWB&PA. Unfortunately, my first official act after that was a wisecrack – “Maybe I should write the Green Wizards’ Standard Monitor, squirrel it here and there, then find it again in a couple lifetimes when the American Renaissance needs fraternal lodges.”
Oh. Boy.
Well… I’ve been doodling a grove rite to ask Hu to bless my rototiller by pulling bits from the Charming of the Plough, borrowing the idea of Oiling Day from the steampunks, and generally being a good magpie… The other holiday I have in mind is the Celebration of All Slain Hope. That’s a Dixieland funeral for our old friend Progress. Six more for a full wheel of the year.
Anyway – I asked my Ogham staves what the most important thing I need to know about this rite is. Ur, Huath, Uilleand. Be patient and plan, I think. A few days later, I asked what I need to understand. Ioho, Duir, Phagos reversed. Looks like I have the ability to do the work, but I’m missing something, not confident, or…? So, I asked what I need to understand about that draw. Huath, Eadha, Koad reversed. Quit being a Cowardly Lion.
Since this is my first attempt, might I ask your opinion? Umm… is asking possible, or is this strictly individual effort?
Anyway, my staves have a really good sense of humor, but talking with them is often like being in a sports bar with my fraternity brothers – kinda hard to remember the good advice when I’m laughing at the bon mot it came wrapped in. Is that normal?
Many thanks.
Rusty

replies.title: Re: Am I doing this rite right?
replies.content:
1) Remember that the best way to see if a ritual works well is to write out a rough draft and then do it a couple of times. You don't have to wait until the canonical day, just walk through it a few times, saying the words and doing the actions, and see how it affects you. Edit it, then set it aside for a few weeks and do the same thing again. Since you won't need it for a couple of lifetimes, why rush? ;-)

2) Some oracles are like that with some people.

replies.title: Re: Precession of the ages
replies.content:
1) This is an essential part of the cycle of human evolution. When the vernal equinox is in Leo and Cancer, the gods and spirits are very close to us; when the vernal equinox is at the other end of the arc, in Aquarius and Capricorn, they are very far away, and we develop our own strength. Our species will go around the whole circle many times -- I suspect the final number will be seven, but that's a guess -- before we finish our period of material incarnation and proceed to other things.

2) As I see it, Aquarius is Uranian, and gives us both the freedom and the burden of working out our own individual relationship to the cosmos, however eccentric that turns out to be. Uranus is a malefic planet, like Saturn, but where Saturn is the lord of limits, Uranus is the lord of liberty: our burden for the next two thousand-odd years is that we get to cope with the consequences of our own actions. After 2160 years of that we'll be ready for the more structured era of Capricorn, and begin the long journey back to the presence of the gods.

3) Of course. It's all part of an initiation.

replies.title: Re: Destiny vs fate

comments.title: Electronic glamor
comments.content:
Update on electronic glamor
Our DVD/Blue-ray player has been having difficulty turning on for a year or more. Power button would have to be pushed a half dozen times for it to come on, player would blink each time but then not come on. mid-january the player doesn't come on at all, a light blinks a few times when the power button is pressed and then goes dark again. Again and again I try and it doesn't work. I resigned myself to knowing that I have to throw this DVD player away. So I disconnect it and take it to the trash, I know I should take it to the recycling center but I took it to the trash instead, and while I'm about to throw it away I notice that there is a exacto knife blade taped to the bottom. I think can it be? May 2018 I taped an exact knife to it. I remove the exacto knife and carefully run a stream of water next to the DVD Blu Ray player next to each of its surfaces figuring that this might dispel bad energies that I have caused it to absorb. I take it back to the living room plug it back in and it works as if it had never had an issue. This issue had been slowly getting worse for a year to a half a year with the DVD player refusing to turn on or turning on then turning off immediately, and eventually if you press the power button enough times it will come on. It has been three weeks since I discovered the blade and removed it and it has continued to work without any sign of the issue.

The LIRP is good; better still is the Middle Pillar exercise, which is a solid invocation; best of all is the whole panoply of Golden Dawn magic, in which you're invoking elements, planets, and divine forces on a regular basis.

I can personally confirm that regular banishings do indeed banish friends, family, and habits from your life--the toxic ones. Because all toxic relationships have an upside, they can be quite addictive, and because loneliness and boredom suck, it usually feels like a loss, so I can totally understand why some people are against banishing.

At the same time, you can't pour fresh water in a full cup. You have to banish the bad to make room for the good. It's amazing how many people want better things in their life, but are too afraid to give up the comfort of worse things. Banishing empties your cup.

If you continue with banishing and especially if you introduce invoking, you'll start to see much more fortunate circumstances, people, and habits, enter your life--but only if you don't give up.

replies.title: Re: Banishing rituals
replies.content:
I don't know Marian Green and I don't happen to know where she got that idea, but there's no shortage of good things in my life, and I've done daily banishing rituals for close to forty years now, so yes, I can confirm that she's wrong.

The effect of "banishing" friends is real, but it comes from two factors. First, if you banish regularly, toxic people get very uncomfortable and leave. Second, if you're engaged in an active process of spiritual development and your friends are not -- even if they're "interested in" spirituality, to the extent of attending coven meetings and the like -- you will drift apart over time, because your interests and needs and concerns will change in ways they can't keep up with. The moral to this story? Make friends who are engaged in the same kind of spiritual practice you are.

replies.title: Re: Question for geomancy
replies.content:
Some authors (Damien Echols is one) are highly opposed to asking direction from an oracle in the form of "should" questions, on the grounds that you're giving away your autonomy to another spirit/power.

It's worth taking a moment to consider what it means to you to make a successful choice. Does it make you happier? Wiser? More Satisfied?

Once you know that, you can ask "Will I be happier for choosing X?" "Will I be wiser for choosing X?" or "Will I be more more satisfied by choosing X?"

The hard part is that you actually have to figure out your values to ask more specific questions. Journaling and reflection can help.

"Also, if you have any other remedies that work for lovesickness, I would appreciate you telling me. I suffer from it."

I've suffered from this myself, twice and badly, but I've experienced a full recovery.

The solution is simple: brutal, heart-wrenching acceptance that you will never be with the person you love. I should add that simple and easy are two different things.

The heart is a silly being that loves who it loves, whether that person is in your life, unattainable, or long past. The good news is that there are an unlimited number of lovers in your imagination that you are fully compatible with.

I find it's much easier to accept unrequited love if you can imagine someone who is compatible with you--and start to notice just how deeply incompatible you are with the IRL person. General training for the imagination can help with this: reading, creative writing, and avoiding tv.

My partner and I both conjured each other after we each suffered a long stint of disappointing relationships, so daydreaming seems to have some magical power as well--as long as you keep looking for your fantasy in real life.

comments.title: Taking care of a nature spot
comments.content:
Hello mr Greer. To preface my question, I am the manager of a large-ish (for my country) nature preserve, which is mostly forests with some meadows and ponds. I take care of it with my team of volunteers as best I can (clearing the paths for visitors, reaping some meadows yearly to avoid brush build-up and favour endangered plant species, doing regular species inventories to evaluate ecosystem health...)
Thus, I'm always wondering if there's something more I could do, or if what I'm doing is right (should I be reaping this parcel for low lying plants or let it grow into a thicket, should I plant a hedgerow here,...). Are you aware of non mundane ways in which I could improve my stewardship of such a place, maybe learn to be more in tune with its needs, and make sure all the species there thrive as best they can?
I hope this doesn't come across as pejorative, as I'm working off the half remembered notion that practitioners of druidism tend to be very focused on nature. I'm not at all initiated in any occult practices, being a scientist by trade, but my mind has been opened to this in part by reading your blog.
Thank you for your writings!

Best regards
JJ

replies.title: Re: Taking care of a nature spot
replies.content: No, none of this comes across as pejorative at all; it's always welcome when a scientist gets past the unfortunate prejudices of the scientific community and deals with the wider world in which we all live. If you want to work with the subtler aspects of nature, however, that takes training and practice. You might consider picking up an introductory book on Druidry -- my book The Druidry Handbook is an example, though my forthcoming book Wisdom of the Oaks might be even better suited to your needs -- and exploring some of the practices in it. Druidry does in fact deal with the spiritual in and through nature, and it's an experiential rather than a dogmatic tradition; you might find it a good fit.

replies.title: Re: Taking care of a nature spot
replies.content: Thank you for the recommendation. Experiential practices do seem best suited to the task at hand, it's after all how most nature conservation is done. replies.title: Re: Taking care of a nature spot
replies.content: LoL, I misread your post; I thought it said ‘maybe learn to be more in tune with its WEEDS...’ Do you happen to pay attention to what weeds grow in which patch? Sorrel likes acidic loam, thistles like sandy soil, etc. Maybe you could draw up a WeedRead Map that shows what the underlying soils are likely to be, based on their preferences. You can use a grid paper in the field, then a spreadsheet to maintain a catalog of who’s where on what parcel, like a gardening chart. Carry litmus strips into the field and sample the soils under a new weed. There might be a way to generate a map using your collected data if it is formatted as GPS locale info. Or, less app-techy, using some kind of raster format based on your spreadsheet data. Observation is, after all, the earliest foundation of all other sciences. Every being likes to be paid some attention. And just using this somewhat neglected technique might cause the plants to ‘speak’ to you in a language you both understand.

replies.title: Re: Taking care of a nature spot
replies.content:

Even though it's unpaid work, I go there at least an hour ever week to hang out and check how things are evolving.
I have maps of the soil composition and geological layers (although there are some surprises, such as unexpected patches of clay in the middle of the limestone).

On a purely scientific level I believe I'm doing all I can with the knowledge available to me, and the most emblematic and endangered species of the preserve are currently in as good a shape as they've ever been in the past few decades (I've only taken over last year). I also have the 50 years of experience of the previous managers I'm still in contact with to draw upon in that regard.

I'm more interested in knowing what I could do that's different and outside of my experience and that of my peer group! Hence my question to mr Greer which he helpfully answered.

replies.title: Re: SOP and energy

comments.title: Dennings and Phillips
comments.content:
I'm curious to know if anyone here has any particular opinion about, or experience with, the magical system set forth by Melita Dennings and Osborne Phillips in their Magical Philosophy series and related works, as expressed in the Aurum Solis and its descendants. I've read through the original volumes and some of the "Llewellyn Practical Guides", as well as some issues of Ogdoadic Journal and Norman Kraft's textbook Ogdoadic Magic. I find the style of it appealing, but am put off by some of the higher level practices, particularly the Constellation of the Worshipped (which sound to me too much like the Wiccan God and Goddess). I understand that the system can be adapted to one's own background, so long as he avoids the "Sub Rosa Nigra" work (which are the specific cult practices of the Aurum Solis and related groups). I am not really interested in formally joining a group or order, but just want to enhance what spiritual life I have now.

Reviews that I have found of this system are all over the map, from its being exactly what D&P say it is (an "Ogdoadic tradition" going back through Victorian times and the Renaissance to ancient cultures), to its being something cobbled together in the 70s by D&P, with the help of Carl Weschcke, as a means of making money off suckers. :( It seems to me a perfectly legitimate way of working, different from similar magical systems like the Golden Dawn and Thelema only in emphasis and detail. Still, I would like some advice from those who know more about this sort of thing than I do.

As for my background in the subject, I grew up in a culture that is steeped in folk magic and magical workings based on Christian ideas (think "Long Lost Friend", but not that exactly), and have experimented with this and that manner of occultism as books (and later, websites) became available. I've always avoided the more advanced or dangerous stuff (except for a frightening TSW experience with sigil magic many years ago!), concentrating on protection, blessings, and seeking what material advantages that The Powers That Be are willing to grant to me, in addition to a conventional prayer life. :) I am a practicing Christian of a rather unorthodox and henotheistic sort, so working with a variety of deities does not intimidate me. Now I feel like I'm ready to start with an organized system from the bottom up, and as I say, the D&P work has in general appealed to me more than most.

Any observations, advice, criticism, or warnings would be quite welcome.

Frater T.O.R. (Taupe Orthagonal Raccoon) ;D

Dear JMG,

replies.title: Re: Brain illness on other planes?
replies.content: I'm sure there are dimensions of epilepsy that extend to the higher planes -- you're right that the name is a dead giveaway -- but I've never seen that point discussed anywhere in modern magical literature, or even in the Renaissance or early modern sources. So I don't really have anything to offer.

replies.title: Re: Brain illness on other planes?
replies.content:
I looked for that, too, at one point in the past, when one of my former students with strong magical interests turned out to have temporal lobe epilepsy. I couldn't find anything in the Medieval texts at my disposal, nor in works from later periods, about why that disease was called "sacred." ( I didn't go through the Greek medical texts surviving from antiquity.)

She often heard voices at inopportune times, disrupting her activities; they were not quite comprehensible, but always threatening in their tone. She wasn't actually scared of her voices all that much, just very deeply annoyed by them. We worked out a simple magical technique to help her cope until she could see a physician about them. It boiled down to her telling them, "I can't pay attention to you right now, but I'll give you time in [however many hours suited her schedule]." It worked: her voices were quite content to remain silent until the appointed time.

A bit of web searching suggested to me that it might be temporal lobe epilepsy, which made finding a physician a little tricky for her. In the state where she lived and planned to spend her adult life, there were stlll fairly severe legal disabilities imposed on anyone ever diagnosed with epilepsy, so it was necessary to find a physician willing to help her medically without recording that as his formal diagnosis. Fortunately, her mother had a physician friend who was willing to do just that and write her up as suffering from migraines rather than epilepsy. She's fine now, and still doing her own sort of magic, which she worked out for herself around her condition. (We're still in sporadic touch, a quarter of a century later.) She's perhaps a small exception to the good general rule that epilepsy and magic don't go well together.

comments.title: Affirmations
comments.content:
Well, I did finish my first one year long one hundred times per day affirmation. I had started it last January in response to the troubling situation at work. I chose, "I feel confident." I found it worked slowly over time. I had a lot of prior experience with mantra and non-discursive meditation, so this was a pretty light practice for me. I usually managed to knock out all one hundred repetitions before work. What was unexpected was that in the last month (?) I could feel the practice gathering force and getting tangibly more powerful, and right when it finished this got even more noticeable. I do feel more confident now, although my life is still a mess. I wish I had started these affirmations years ago.

I started a second affirmation two months after I had started the first one (I didn't want to do two affirmations at once, but partly due to the lockdown and being unable to work, and partly due to the positive effects of the first affirmation creating a feedback loop) for increased patience that is still ongoing. Of the two, patience is definitely more lacking in me, so I don't expect as dramatic a change from this one, but I'm open to being pleasantly surprised. I will be glad when it's finished.

My question is, I noticed that my automatic thinking tends to be heavily skewed towards the negative, whether about my own life, or the world, or random stuff like old TV shows, it doesn't matter. I want experience more positive thinking. Would, "I have positive thoughts," be good, or "I'm thinking positively," or "I think positive" be good? I know you have said before that it's good not to force yourself to thinking positive all the time, but I assume thinking negative all the time isn't good either. My aim is mainly to change my involuntary thoughts, I still want to be able to consider negative situations an possibilities when I'm thinking deliberately.

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Affirmations
replies.content: In your case I'd probably do something a little more nuanced, such as "my thoughts always turn toward the positive." Still, your mileage may vary.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, but I find Bayesian reasoning an extremely helpful means of approaching some problems. It basically boils down to probability. Rather than "this is absolutely right/wrong" you are wagering "What are the chances x-hypothesis is correct?" What are the chances the sun rises tomorrow? Instead of saying "it's absolutely certain the sun will rise tomorrow," the mindset is "given what we know about past events, it's highly probable that the sun will rise tomorrow." Thus, a better scientist admits that what he or she does is theorize, and that theories are just what they are--educated guesses, based on accrued evidence. This is why it's called the theory of evolution, the germ theory of disease, etc. All these are accepted as our best guesses--until you get contrary evidence. At least, this is my extremely dumbed-down version of it.

It's also why it's unclear if materialists have anything to say about the nature of the gods: if physics etc. are purely theories related to the natural world, how can it addressed things that are supernatural?

I think this skeptical, generally humble and conjecture-based mindset is useful in life. So probably extends to magic, as well.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Examples of how the sun could potentially not rise tomorrow:

- It's polar night.
- It's polar day. The sun can't rise if it didn't set.
- A biblical miracle occurs. The sun again stands still over Gibeon, and therefore doesn't rise over your hometown.
- The sun has been in the process of being slowly consumed by a stray black hole gravitationally stuck in its center. Tonight is the time we find out about it.
- Aliens Death Star the sun overnight and make it go supernova.
- A critical glitch in the matrix of space-time causes it to fail to rise.
- The universe as a whole comes to an end.

When we say "the sun will rise tomorrow," what we are implicitly saying is "according to my latitude, the time of year, and the currently-accepted laws of physics, the sun will rise tomorrow, and since I have no reason to believe that those laws are invalid or that they are at imminent risk of changing, I can say this with a very high degree of confidence."

comments.title: SOP and the Tree of Life

replies.title: Re: SOP and the Tree of Life
replies.content: The SoP works with a different map. Do you recall the distinction between solar, telluric, and lunar currents, and their associated traditions? The Tree of Life is a solar diagram, dealing with the descent of energies from above, and the whole structure of GD magic is set up to work with that. There are also telluric traditions and magical methods -- RJ Stewart's books are good sources for this. Then there are lunar traditions and methods, which combine the solar and telluric currents, and so by definition draw energies from two directions, the below as well as the above. That takes a different map. I've sketched out a tentative suggestion for what that map might be in The Druid Magic Handbook, but it'll probably take a few centuries or the discovery of some obscure literature to get something really suited.

comments.title: Magic and Crypto Currency
comments.content: Although national fiat currency isn't much better, it at least in theory is backed (to some measurable extent) by the labor and economic output of a given nation. But I'm puzzled by the success of crypto currencies. Bitcoin blasted through 40k this morning when Tesla said they dropped 1.5 billion dollars into it. I'm sitting here wondering how so many people can believe this abstraction has the value that it does. I guess this applies to equities too: Tesla has a Price to Earnings Ratio of 1,358.56, which suggests a mega-bubble (Apple is 36.88 in contrast). It is as if millions have fallen into a spell and are happily handing over their life savings into a swirling void. Do you think some sort of spellwork is active?

replies.title: Re: Magic and Crypto Currency
replies.content:
Economist here: no need to assume that. When you have lots of money chasing after things with little return, that's the classic recipe for a speculative bubble. Especially when a lot of the big players are confident that the Fed will bail them out: heads, they win; tails, they don't lose. When you have lots of people with lots of money confident they can take whatever risks and have no consequences, the results are strange; the Bitcoin and Tesla bubbles are only one of the many symptoms of this.

A lot of the money pouring into Bitcoin, or Tesla, or whatever, is by people who don't bother to think about risk, because they are confident that it doesn't matter. So of course the results are absurd: take away risk and the markets break down.

That said, you're correct, of course -- there's no need for occultists to cast illusion spells on investors when investors are so willing to do the work themselves!

comments.title: Magic is Personal
comments.content:
Good afternoon.
After reading your 'blogs, books, and these articles here, and over the last two years I did a more in-depth reading of Druidry websites, I concluded that all these years, since my religiosity diverged from Christianity and monotheism, I've been a druid all along.
When you wrote of the LBRP, and it's adaptations and modifications, I spent a lot of time trying to work with it every morning, adjusting, mutating, and refining it. What I determined was that, while there was an effect, I didn't really feel it.
After I signed onto the AODA, and began to work through the curriculum they sent and the course you laid out in A Druidry Handbook in earnest, I began by learning the Sphere of Protection every morning instead of doing the LBRP. Of course, presented with multiple suggested examples built around the basic structure, I very quickly developed my own (mostly what I could actually remember when doing the ritual -- if I can't remember it, then it's not suitable) and found that the difference between the two morning rituals was quite astounding.
I perform the SOP in the morning and immediately feel positively energized, awake (I'm NOT a 'morning person') and ready to face the day. The rest of the day, I find myself as focused as I can ever be, and better able to bring my usually scattered thoughts down to earth and able to do whatever it is I need to do, like cooking breakfast without burning something, or finishing a leather project, or being more focused at work.
I find that trying to call specific historical deities didn't really suit me because I've never really felt drawn to any particular pantheon and, to be honest, trying to find four deities with four-letter names was a bit hard. But speaking directly to the powers of nature, unnamed, and their icons, the visualization, which the study materials describe as giving many people difficulty, I found came naturally to me, probably because I've spent so much time outdoors that I've experienced all the various scenes multiple times during the years. I've had deer bound out of the woods on the trail in front of me, I've seen a hawk flying out of the morning sun across a lake, and so on.
So, I suppose this is not really a question, per se, but more of a discourse on the idea that magical ritual must be inherently personal in it's expression to be effective. The only aspect that I haven't really settled is whether there is a set of principles which might help individuals to find what resonates with them more quickly than it took me. If there was a process, beyond the tedious, time-consuming, and potentially discouraging trial-and-error to find what really calls to one.
What I conclude is that teaching discursive meditation as a preliminary would probably be the best way for a new practitioner to proceed, beginning with the question, what do I like? Robes and ornaments and theatrical ritual, or just being in the woods, or both? (The most powerful rituals I've run have been in secluded groves.) Is there a pantheon that calls? or just the idea of Gods that, as yet, have no name? Or Gods who haven't revealed their names?
Certainly learning about discursive meditation from you and the AODA has given me a wonderful tool for discovery that I wish I'd learned about decades ago.

I'd also like to comment on the work you've done over the past 30+ years, first by grounding the AODA firmly in this world and, I suspect, playing a significant role in making sure that the organization doesn't end up like ADF or any of the very many defunct "organizations" that turn out to be just a couple dabblers with a love of nature and a cobwebsite, if their site still exists at all. I think your influence will ripple out very far into the aether indeed.

Bruce
(AKA Renaissance Man)

Aquarius is an Air sign, ruled by Uranus; as I read it, it's less about change and more about eccentricity and individualism, and so notions that call for everyone to march together toward some grand goal have little appeal to the Aquarian energy.

comments.title: My First Magic Monday Question of 2021!

replies.title: Re: My First Magic Monday Question of 2021!
replies.content: The classic exercise is to spend five minutes a day just imagining things. For example, spend five minutes imagining a place you've visited, or a walk in your neighborhood, or someplace that doesn't exist (for example, a street scene in Hobbiton); make it as clear and vivid as you can, and include other senses -- hear the sounds, smell the odors, etc. Do this daily and over time your ability to visualize will improve a great deal. (It worked for me.)

comments.title: Synesthesia
comments.content:
For JMG and/or the commentariat:

I have a mild sound-to-color synesthesia: certain sounds register visually as a brief flash of light. Until recently, it was always white, and the sounds that typically provoked it were unanticipated clicking, popping, or tapping type noises.

Until recently, I had no reason to doubt the prevalent theories about this: crossed wires, sensory connections the brain failed to prune in childhood.

My current toddler has me rethinking it. He's not my first kid, but for whatever reason he, and not his siblings, can register his unhappiness in color. When he wakes in the night, his cries flash by in vivid green and orange, along with a faint tactile sensation, like being hit in the face. All still consistent with the neurological phenomena I know, but odd that it changed color, after all these years.

The last few weeks, we've started moving him to his own bed, which is snugged up next to ours. He'd been sharing with us, but he's getting too big and kicky, so we make a big deal out of putting him to bed, singing songs, rubbing his back, etc. etc. and he is gradually getting the idea. He's not happy about it, but it's progress. When he wakes in the night, we comfort him and put him back to bed. At some point I realized that when I put him back in his bed, I was still getting the green and orange flashes, and the tactile sensation on my face... even though he wasn't crying. He was still quite unhappy, but there was no sound to provoke the thing.

My toddler, convinced not to cry, is still broadcasting his displeasure on other wavelengths. Clearly, there's more to it than the old sound=color thing that I'm used to.

1) Any chance you or the commentariat might have some insight into this? It seems like we're not in strictly-neurological territory any more.
2) If so, is there some way to muffle it? It's fairly unpleasant, particularly at 2am.
3) Is this the sort of thing that goes away with weaning, maybe? Hopefully?

replies.title: Re: Synesthesia
replies.content:
1) It's neurological, so long as you recognize that telepathy is a neurological phenomenon!

2) I'm sorry to say parent-child telepathy, like sibling telepathy, is very hard to muffle. It works at a deep biological level and goes right past just about anything the conscious mind can do.

3) Once your toddler gets used to sleeping alone he should stop doing it.

replies.title: Re: Synesthesia
replies.content:
Thanks! That's roughly how I grokked it, it's good to have a second opinion confirm.

3)I look forward to it!

Hi, JMG- hope you had a relaxing and restorative month away! I gave it a go upon finishing the chapter, and while it felt a bit clumsy as the first few times of a ritual always do, I was pretty bowled over by the amount of energy it seemed to generate (I've read commenters here referring to performing ritual as feeling like a workout, but it's the first time I've ever had the experience). For background, I've been doing the regular Middle Pillar exercise daily for between one and two years now- not exactly sure how long, though I suppose I could check my journal. I'm planning on working with the curriculum you suggested here awhile back of starting with the R+C ritual done weekly, then moving on to learning to open and close a temple, and working through the greater rituals of the Pentagram and Hexagram weekly, moving through the elements and planets and meditating on the results. So far I've spent about a month with the Rose Cross, but haven't yet started opening a temple. Do you have any thoughts for integrating the expanded Middle Pillar into this structure?

Thanks much!
-NPM

replies.title: Re: Great Middle Pillar Ritual
replies.content:
Good -- that's a solid sequence of practices. I'd recommend the Greater MP exercise once a week. Once you're used to the energy, you can step things up by doing it as a composite ritual:

1. Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram
2. Greater Invoking Ritual of the Pentagram
3. Greater Middle Pillar exercise
4. Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram

This will boost the effect, and it will also help you get ready for major rituals.
